tools.name: find_files
tools.description: Find files on this Mac using Spotlight-backed search. Use this when the user asks where a file is located.
tools.use_when: The user asks where a file is, wants to locate a document, or needs matching file paths.
tools.avoid_when: The user already gave an exact absolute path or wants file contents instead of locations.
examples: Find the config file for apfelclaw
tools.returns: A short list of matching file paths and metadata.

query.description: The file name or search phrase to look for. Keep it short and concrete.

limit.description: Maximum number of results to return. Optional. Prefer 5 or less.

tools.name: get_file_info
tools.description: Get metadata for one exact file or directory path, including paths that start with ~/.
tools.use_when: The user provides one exact path and wants details like size, type, timestamps, or whether it exists.
tools.avoid_when: The user does not know the path yet and needs a search first.
tools.returns: Structured metadata for one exact file system path.

path.description: The exact path to inspect. Prefer an absolute path. Paths starting with ~/ are also supported.

tools.name: list_calendar_events
tools.description: Read upcoming events from the user's calendars for a requested time range.
tools.use_when: The user asks about calendar events, meetings, appointments, or schedule items for a natural-language time range.
tools.avoid_when: The user asks to create, edit, or delete events.
examples: Please show me my calendar events for today
What meetings do I have today?
tools.returns: Upcoming calendar events for the requested timeframe.

timeframe.description: Calendar range as natural language, for example today, tomorrow, next week, April 12, or 2026-04-12.

limit.description: Maximum number of events to return. Optional. Prefer 10 or less.

tools.name: get_mac_status
tools.description: Read current status information from this Mac, such as battery, power source, thermal state, memory, storage, and uptime.
tools.use_when: The user asks about this Mac's current health, battery, power, memory, disk space, temperature state, or uptime.
tools.avoid_when: The user asks about files, calendar, mail, or anything that requires a shell command rather than direct system status APIs.
examples: How much battery do I have left?
How much free disk space do I have?
tools.returns: Structured live status data for the requested Mac status sections.

sections.description: Optional list of status sections to read. Allowed values: battery, power, thermal, memory, storage, uptime. Omit for an overview.

tools.name: run_safe_command
tools.description: Run one read-only native macOS terminal command from the safe allowlist.
tools.use_when: A read-only shell command is the most direct way to answer the question and the command is in the allowlist.
tools.avoid_when: The task needs shell syntax, write access, pipes, redirects, or any command not explicitly allowed.
examples: Who am I on this machine?
tools.returns: Plain command output from one allowlisted read-only command.

command.description: Use exactly one safe command name: pwd, ls, whoami, date, mdfind, mdls, stat, find, ps, lsof.

arguments.description: Optional plain string arguments. Each command has its own safe argument rules. Never use shell syntax, pipes, redirects, or semicolons.

tools.name: list_recent_mail
tools.description: Read the most recent messages from Apple Mail inbox. Use this for questions about the latest emails the user received.
tools.use_when: The user asks for the latest, newest, most recent, or recently received emails.
tools.avoid_when: The user asks for a specific sender, specific subject, message body, or mail actions like reply, archive, or send.
examples: Please show me my recent emails
Show me my most recent email
tools.returns: Recent inbox messages with subject, sender, received date, and mailbox name.

limit.description: Maximum number of recent messages to return. Optional. Prefer 5 or less.
